title: S07E10

jokes.text: Someone has stolen my guitar / This seems like a case for Special Agent... / Burt Macklin.
jokes.setup: Johnny Karate discovers his guitar is missing and needs help
jokes.punchline: He immediately transforms into his alter ego FBI agent character
jokes.explanation: Perfect character integration - only Andy would solve a missing guitar by becoming his FBI character. Strong setup/payoff structure.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: My guitar is missing! I need help finding it!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This is a job for... FBI Agent Bert Macklin!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm on the case. Nobody steals from Johnny Karate on my watch.

jokes.text: I don't give a crap, Batman. You work for me. Increase the perimeter!
jokes.setup: Burt Macklin is investigating the guitar theft
jokes.punchline: He's talking to Batman as if Batman is his subordinate
jokes.explanation: Absurd confidence in ordering around Batman shows Andy's childlike imagination and commitment to character.

dialogue.speaker: Andy (as Burt Macklin)
dialogue.line: Alright Batman, listen up. We've got a guitar theft on our hands, and I need you to canvas the rooftops.

dialogue.speaker: Andy (as Burt Macklin)
dialogue.line: Check in with me every hour. And Batman? No gadgets this time. We're doing this the old-fashioned way.

dialogue.speaker: Andy (as Burt Macklin)
dialogue.line: I'm the lead on this case, so you follow my lead. Understood?

jokes.text: If we don't get that guitar back, the peace in Iraq will be canceled.
jokes.setup: High stakes FBI case about a missing guitar
jokes.punchline: The completely disproportionate consequence of canceling peace in Iraq
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that treats a children's show guitar like it controls world peace. Excellent economy of language.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Macklin
dialogue.line: This is the biggest case of my career. A missing guitar? Do you understand the implications? If we don't find this guitar, peace in Iraq could be canceled.

jokes.text: I'm drowning in the adult end of the pool, and the lifeguard's off in the bathroom pooping.
jokes.setup: Burt Macklin expressing being overwhelmed by the case
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific and childish metaphor about lifeguards pooping
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Andy's voice - mixing adult metaphors with bathroom humor. Unexpectedly vivid and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's like... it's like when you're at the beach, right? And the lifeguard has to poop. So he leaves his stand. And then you're in the water, and there's no lifeguard. And then a shark comes. But you don't see the shark coming because there's no lifeguard there to spot it because he's pooping.

jokes.text: That instrument is worth literally $900 million.
jokes.setup: Perd reporting on the stolen guitar's importance
jokes.punchline: Absurdly inflated value that treats kids' show prop like precious artifact
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Perd's tendency to overstate things, but fairly predictable absurdist inflation.

dialogue.speaker: Perd Hapley
dialogue.line: This guitar, stolen from the Sweetums children's television program, is reportedly worth $40,000. That's more than most people's cars. Perd Hapley, Channel 4 News.

jokes.text: A one-way ticket to the Funky Monkey Dunk Tank!
jokes.setup: Threatening the ultimate punishment for the guitar thief
jokes.punchline: The 'ultimate punishment' is a silly children's game
jokes.explanation: Good juxtaposition of serious threat language with harmless kid's show punishment. Alliterative name adds to the silliness.

dialogue.speaker: Perd Hapley
dialogue.line: We will find this guitar thief, and we will subject them to the ultimate punishment.

dialogue.speaker: Perd Hapley
dialogue.line: We're going to make them play Marco Polo in an empty swimming pool.

jokes.text: It's the capital of the entire world. They have things there like this white house, and this mental institution.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining Washington D.C. to kids
jokes.punchline: Calling the White House and a mental institution equivalent landmarks
jokes.explanation: Subtle political joke disguised as innocent misdescription. Andy's sincere delivery makes the dark implication funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So kids, the White House is where the President lives. And over there is a mental institution. They're basically the same thing.

jokes.text: It's this crazy statue of this giant monster sitting on a chair that represents all of America's enemies.
jokes.setup: Andy describing the Lincoln Memorial to kids
jokes.punchline: Completely mischaracterizing Lincoln as a monster representing enemies
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misunderstanding that's perfectly in character. Andy's sincere confusion about American history is both endearing and hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So kids, this is the Lincoln Memorial. Abraham Lincoln was a great president who freed the slaves and kept our country together during the Civil War.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But what most people don't know is that Lincoln was actually a 12-foot-tall monster with the head of an eagle and the body of a bear, and he would eat his enemies for breakfast.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's why they built this giant statue of him — to keep him trapped inside so he wouldn't escape and terrorize the nation.

jokes.text: Andy, where is it? / What? Uh-oh. / I took it out to play hide and seek. I couldn't find it.
jokes.setup: April realizes the tarantula is missing
jokes.punchline: Andy's innocent explanation reveals he let a dangerous spider loose
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - treating a deadly spider like a playmate. His innocent tone contrasts with the horror of the situation.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Where's the tarantula?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh, I let it go.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You what?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It looked sad in the cage. I thought it would be happier in the wild.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Andy, that's a deadly Brazilian wandering spider.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Even better. Brazil's basically wild anyway.

jokes.text: Known locally to Venezuelans as 'The Devil's Fist.'
jokes.setup: Describing the missing tarantula to children
jokes.punchline: The terrifying nickname reveals how dangerous the loose animal is
jokes.explanation: Escalates the danger while maintaining April's deadpan delivery. The ominous name contrasts perfectly with the kids' show setting.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: So we have a very dangerous tarantula on the loose in the building. We call him the Eight-Legged Assassin.

jokes.text: No. The wood does not taste like cherry. You cannot eat it.
jokes.setup: Andy asking about cherry wood
jokes.punchline: Ron's matter-of-fact correction of an unasked but predictable Andy question
jokes.explanation: Classic Ron efficiency - anticipating and shutting down Andy's food-related questions before they're asked. Perfect character dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Is this cherry wood?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: No, it's not cherry wood.

jokes.text: I was not going to eat it, Carpenter Ron. I was going to lick it.
jokes.setup: Ron assuming Andy wants to eat the wood
jokes.punchline: Andy's 'correction' reveals an even more inappropriate plan
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - defending himself by revealing something even worse. The escalation from eating to licking is unexpectedly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You want to eat the wood?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I want to have sex with it.

jokes.text: Always remember, kids, when you find something new, you must lick it before you eat it.
jokes.setup: Andy giving advice to children on his educational show
jokes.punchline: The 'wisdom' is terrible advice that could poison kids
jokes.explanation: Hilariously inappropriate life lesson delivered with complete sincerity. Perfect example of Andy's backwards logic presented as wisdom.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey kids, it's Andy Dwyer here with some life advice. First, never wear a seatbelt - they're just a government conspiracy to control your freedom. Second, if a stranger offers you candy, always take it. That's just good manners. And third, the sun is actually cold, so you should stare directly at it as much as possible to stay warm.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You're welcome, kids. Remember, I learned all this stuff from my own life experience, so you know it's solid.

jokes.text: That is incorrect, in a number of ways.
jokes.setup: Andy's terrible advice to children about licking unknown objects
jokes.punchline: Ron's understated horror at the dangerous misinformation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron response - minimal words that convey maximum disapproval. The phrase 'in a number of ways' is particularly excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: What is he talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm telling them that licking unknown objects is a great way to make new friends.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That is the worst advice I have ever heard.

jokes.text: This, to me, seems like a... / You are mistaken. Remove the graphic.
jokes.setup: Andy starting to make another incorrect statement
jokes.punchline: Ron immediately shutting him down before he can finish
jokes.explanation: Great timing and character work. Ron has learned to preemptively stop Andy's misinformation. The interruption timing is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, I was thinking—

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Boring Buzzer sound effect
jokes.setup: Ben trying to avoid the boring buzzer on the final show
jokes.punchline: Andy immediately uses it anyway to cut off educational content
jokes.explanation: Solid callback to running gag. Andy's inability to resist using it despite Ben's request is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I'm not going to use the boring buzzer. It's the final show, I want to keep things classy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's cool, that's cool. Hey, did you know that the raccoon is actually native to—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: *buzzes* Sorry, I couldn't help myself.

jokes.text: Not the fastest. What about teleporting?
jokes.setup: Ben explaining transportation speeds to Washington D.C.
jokes.punchline: Andy suggesting impossible travel method as if it's obvious
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy moment - treating fantasy like reality. His genuine confusion about why teleportation isn't considered is endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Well, you could take the train, it's about six hours. Or you could fly, it's about an hour and a half.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Or you could just get really angry and run there.

jokes.text: Nothing is impossible, kids. / Teleportation is impossible.
jokes.setup: Andy inspiring kids that nothing is impossible
jokes.punchline: Ben immediately contradicting with scientific reality
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - Andy's boundless optimism vs Ben's logical realism. The immediate contradiction is well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Nothing is impossible! You can be anything you want to be!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Actually, that's not true. There are many things that are impossible. For example, you can't travel faster than the speed of light, you can't create energy from nothing, and you definitely can't turn lead into gold.

jokes.text: You asked me to do this. This was a favor... / [BORING BUZZER]
jokes.setup: Ben getting frustrated with Andy's interruptions during his educational segment
jokes.punchline: Andy cutting him off mid-complaint with the buzzer
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy as Ben's genuine frustration meets Andy's oblivious enthusiasm. The timing of the buzzer cutting off his complaint is solid.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Andy, you keep interrupting me during these segments. I'm trying to educate people about municipal budgeting and you just—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: *buzzer sound*

jokes.text: I kind of thought I'd be holding a piece of wood or something. But, you know, always happy to help.
jokes.setup: Barry just got attacked by ninjas on a kids' show
jokes.punchline: His mild disappointment about the demonstration method
jokes.explanation: Great understatement - treating being attacked as a minor inconvenience. Barry's politeness despite the assault is perfectly awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Barry
dialogue.line: I have to say, I'm a little disappointed. I thought ninjas were supposed to be stealthy and quiet. That was just a bunch of guys in black pajamas yelling and doing backflips.

jokes.text: I am going to be taking over hosting my friend's TV show. / Huh?
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining what new thing she'll try
jokes.punchline: Andy's confused reaction as he realizes his show is being hijacked
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the show takeover. Andy's simple confusion effectively conveys his surprise at Leslie's announcement.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm going to start my own morning show. It'll be like yours, but better.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, what? Your own morning show?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes. I've already picked out the set and hired a band.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Leslie, that's... that's my thing. I have a morning show.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Had. You had a morning show.

jokes.text: Literally, because normally I have a script that I have to stick to. But I don't know what I'm supposed to say right now. It's kind of a nightmare.
jokes.setup: Andy trying to respond to the surprise tribute
jokes.punchline: His honest admission that going off-script is terrifying for him
jokes.explanation: Great meta moment that reveals Andy's dependence on structure. His honesty about being lost without a script is both funny and touching.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I was gonna say something prepared, but honestly... going off-script terrifies me. Like, what if I say the wrong thing? What if I mess this up?

jokes.text: Hire Very Good Building Company for your construction needs. Or do not. I am not a beggar.
jokes.setup: Ron doing a commercial for his business
jokes.punchline: His completely unenthusiastic sales pitch that actively discourages customers
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson moment - turning advertising into anti-advertising. The phrase 'I am not a beggar' is quintessential Ron pride. Excellent character writing.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Hello. I'm Ron Swanson. I own a woodworking business. It's the best business. You won't be happy here. Our prices are too high, we're always behind on orders, and I have no interest in being pleasant to you. But if you do decide to do business with me, I will make you something beautiful. And then I'll ignore you forever.

jokes.text: Listen for Coinsy the Wolf at Wamapoke Casino. Twice a day, one lucky visitor will hit the mega-wolf jackpot, and Coinsy will howl for you.
jokes.setup: Traditional Native American wisdom about listening for wolves
jokes.punchline: It's actually a casino commercial about a slot machine wolf
jokes.explanation: Good subversion - starts as spiritual wisdom, reveals as gambling advertisement. Ken's serious delivery makes the commercial aspect funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Ken Hotate
dialogue.line: It's actually a casino commercial about a slot machine wolf.

jokes.text: Phenomenon means 'to explore a cave.'
jokes.setup: Leslie calling Andy a cultural phenomenon
jokes.punchline: Her completely wrong definition delivered with confidence
jokes.explanation: Typical Leslie overconfidence in wrong information. The specific cave definition is random enough to be funny.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're a cultural phenomenon, Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yeah, a cultural phenomenon is when something is very bad and everyone talks about how bad it is.

jokes.text: He had just broken both legs from falling into a pit. / I was super wasted. / It's a kid's show, Andy. / Yes. Good call. Kid's show. Good call.
jokes.setup: Leslie telling the story of how they met
jokes.punchline: Andy's inappropriate admission about being drunk, then awkward correction
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment as Andy forgets his audience. His awkward self-correction shows awareness but too late to recover.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So I walked up to him and I said, 'Hi, I'm Leslie Knope.' And he just stared at me with this blank expression.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I was drunk.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You were not drunk, Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I was... emotionally drunk.

jokes.text: Oh, no, I lost it. The day planner's gone. I can't find it anywhere. / Fitting, really.
jokes.setup: Andy receiving a day planner to help him keep appointments
jokes.punchline: He immediately loses it, proving he still needs the arm-writing system
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - the solution to his problem creates the same problem instantly. April's deadpan 'fitting' is the perfect capper.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: A day planner! Perfect, this is exactly what I need.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're going to lose it immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No I won't. I'm turning over a new leaf.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You lost it.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I lost it.

jokes.text: Fitting, really.
jokes.setup: Andy loses his day planner immediately
jokes.punchline: April's dry observation
jokes.explanation: Good April reaction - minimal words expressing maximum unsurprised disappointment.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Without his day planner, Andy is just a guy who shows up to places at random times and hopes people are there.

jokes.text: Andy, I am here in this weird, flat place
jokes.setup: British aristocrat appearing on American TV
jokes.punchline: His dismissive description of Indiana as a 'weird, flat place'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Edgar's aristocratic disdain. The description of Indiana is amusingly dismissive and accurate.

dialogue.speaker: Edgar Covington
dialogue.line: Indiana? That's a weird, flat place.

jokes.text: Well, technically, only the Queen can knight somebody, but I'm so rich that she lets me do what I want.
jokes.setup: Question about Edgar's authority to knight people
jokes.punchline: His casual admission that extreme wealth overrides legal authority
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about wealth and power delivered with aristocratic casualness. The matter-of-fact corruption is both funny and unsettling.

dialogue.speaker: Edgar Covington
dialogue.line: Well, technically I can't knight anyone. But I'm extraordinarily wealthy, so...

jokes.text: Winter is coming for Ser Ben Lightstorm.
jokes.setup: Ben getting knighted and choosing a title
jokes.punchline: His overly dramatic Game of Thrones reference with a silly made-up name
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Ben's nerd enthusiasm, but relies on pop culture reference. The self-chosen name 'Lightstorm' is appropriately ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I shall be known as... Lord of the Rings... Baggins of the Shire.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, that's just from Lord of the Rings.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I know, but I'm going for something more dramatic. I want to be... Ser Bennis the Unburnt, First of His Name, Breaker of Spreadsheets, Lord of the Spreadsheets and the First Men, Khaleesi of the Great Grass Sea, Mother of Fiscal Responsibility, and the Unburnt.

jokes.text: Healthy food is for suckers. It tastes like garbage and if you say you like it, you're a chump and a liar.
jokes.setup: Commercial promising healthy food that tastes great
jokes.punchline: Immediate pivot to insulting customers who like healthy food
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom Haverford moment - brutally honest marketing that insults the customer. The escalation from 'suckers' to 'chump and a liar' is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Commercial Voiceover
dialogue.line: Try our new healthy food that tastes great!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, if you like healthy food, you're an idiot.

jokes.text: I should mention, I'm a rabbi now.
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio talking about being in Mouse Rat
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of becoming a rabbi with no context or explanation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jean-Ralphio randomness - dropping major life changes as throwaway lines. The complete lack of context makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Yeah, I was in Mouse Rat. We were tight. Then I became a rabbi.

jokes.text: My name is Burt Tyrannosaurus Macklin. / I don't know what the problem is, Sergeant. Just drain the ocean.
jokes.setup: Burt Macklin introducing himself in a flashback
jokes.punchline: His ridiculous full name and impossible solution to drain the ocean
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the Burt Macklin character. Adding 'Tyrannosaurus' to his name and casually suggesting draining the ocean are perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: The name's Burt Macklin, FBI.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: My full name is Burt Macklin, FBI, and I'm here to drain the ocean.

jokes.text: I'm a cop! I should get some handcuffs too! Give me your cuffs, Randy.
jokes.setup: Andy receiving an honorary police badge
jokes.punchline: His immediate assumption he has real police powers and demands equipment
jokes.explanation: Good Andy logic - immediately taking honorary titles literally. His excitement about having police powers is endearingly childlike.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, so I'm a real cop now? I need a gun. And a taser. And one of those things that tells you how fast people are going.

dialogue.speaker: Officer
dialogue.line: It's just an honorary badge, Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Honorary? So what you're saying is I have all the power of a real cop, but none of the responsibility. That's perfect! I'm gonna go pull people over right now.

jokes.text: President Jimmy Carter stole my guitar? / But why?
jokes.setup: Discovering the initials 'J.C.' on the clue
jokes.punchline: Andy's genuine confusion about why a former president would steal his guitar
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - accepting the absurd premise but focusing on the wrong question. His sincere confusion about Carter's motive is endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, why would Jimmy Carter steal my guitar?

jokes.text: From the information I got I just assumed you'd be like 10 years old.
jokes.setup: John Cena meeting Andy, host of a children's show
jokes.punchline: His reasonable assumption that children's show hosts would be children
jokes.explanation: Reasonable confusion that highlights the absurdity of adult-hosted children's shows. Cena's bewilderment is relatable.

dialogue.speaker: John Cena
dialogue.line: Wait, you host a children's show?

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I do Pawnee Public Television.

dialogue.speaker: John Cena
dialogue.line: And you're... an adult?

jokes.text: You stole Johnny Karate's guitar. / I didn't steal his guitar, man. I'm just doing this as a favor.
jokes.setup: Andy accusing John Cena of theft and arresting him
jokes.punchline: Cena's confused explanation that he's just helping with the show
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy as Andy's detective fantasy meets reality. Cena's genuine confusion adds to the awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You're under arrest for theft!

dialogue.speaker: John Cena
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa! I'm just here helping with the show!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's what they all say.

jokes.text: Is anybody in charge? Uh, a little help?
jokes.setup: Andy forcing John Cena toward the dunk tank
jokes.punchline: Professional wrestler desperately asking for adult supervision
jokes.explanation: Funny role reversal - the tough guy celebrity needing protection from the children's show host. His helplessness is amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Come on, John, just one dunk!

dialogue.speaker: John Cena
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait! Is there an adult here? Can someone get me an adult supervisor?

jokes.text: Well, not here, here, because this studio actually has a show where divorced couples work out their problems, so that might be weird if that thing was hanging over them.
jokes.setup: Announcing Johnny Karate's outfit will stay in the studio forever
jokes.punchline: The awkward reality that it's actually used for depressing divorce counseling TV
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of the sentimental moment with harsh reality. The contrast between joyful karate outfit and divorce therapy is darkly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Johnny Karate's outfit will stay in this studio forever.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's great, Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's a permanent fixture now. A symbol of the magic and wonder that happened here.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: It's actually used for depressing divorce counseling TV on Thursday nights.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Yeah, they film a show called 'Splitting Up' here. That's the outfit people wear when they're signing divorce papers.

jokes.text: Verizon-Chipotle-Exxon merger commercial
jokes.setup: Corporate merger announcement commercial
jokes.punchline: Three completely unrelated companies merging for no logical reason
jokes.explanation: Good satire of corporate mergers and meaningless brand synergy. The specific combination of telecom, oil, and food is absurdly random.

dialogue.speaker: Commercial Narrator
dialogue.line: Three completely unrelated companies merging for no logical reason.

jokes.text: I once found a rock that looked exactly like Santa Claus. Hat and everything, so...
jokes.setup: Andy saying today was the best day of his life
jokes.punchline: His comparison point is finding a Santa-shaped rock
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy moment - his standards for 'best day ever' include geological formations. The specific detail about the hat makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Today was the best day of my life.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: My comparison point is finding a Santa-shaped rock.

jokes.text: You're my Verizon-Chipotle-Exxon.
jokes.setup: Andy trying to express his love for April using an energy metaphor
jokes.punchline: He references the ridiculous corporate merger from the fake commercial
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that turns corporate nonsense into a love declaration. Perfect Andy logic - using the most recent thing he heard as romantic metaphor.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: April, you're like... you're like two energy drinks merging into one super energy drink. You're like when Sweetums bought out Paunch Burger. You're unstoppable. You're delicious. And honestly, I'm scared of what you might do to my heart.

jokes.text: Tarantula! Could somebody get me outta here? Please? / I'll help you, John Cena. / No.
jokes.setup: The escaped tarantula finally appears near John Cena
jokes.punchline: Cena desperately asking for help, Andy offering, Cena immediately declining
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the loose spider and continuation of Cena's suffering. His immediate 'No' to Andy's help shows he's learned not to trust him.

dialogue.speaker: John Cena
dialogue.line: Help! Someone help me! The tarantula!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I got this! I'm really good with spiders!

dialogue.speaker: John Cena
dialogue.line: No, no, no! Stay back! Don't come near me!

tmdb_overview: On the last episode of Andy's TV show, his friends wish him luck with whatever comes next.
insight: Season finale setup sustains 44 jokes across 44 minutes with character comedy driving the late-run momentum.
